01 — ABOUT

From sketch to spec sheet.

I like hardware that has to actually work — not just render well.

I'm a Mechanical Engineering student at Georgia Tech (Minor in Industrial Design, Class of 2027) with a habit of taking on more builds than I probably should. Most of that time lives in SolidWorks: tolerancing enclosures so they print clean, modeling drivetrains, and figuring out how a part should be manufactured, not just how it looks from one angle.

Outside class, I design robot shells for Georgia Tech's RoboCup team and build event hardware at TAPN, where I've shipped check-in kiosks and RFID systems under budgets a tenth the size of commercial equivalents. I also just build things for fun — a 3D-printed replica FIFA trophy, a hand-crank music box, an assistive device for someone who actually needed one.

02 — CASE STUDIES

Three projects, three problems.

Each sheet below follows the same path: the problem I was handed, how I approached it in CAD, and what actually shipped.

SHEET 01 OF 03 — ONGOING

RoboCup Shell Redesign

TEAMRoboJackets
ROLEMechanical Team Member
TIMELINEJan 2026 – Present
TOOLSSolidWorks, Inventor
Problem

Six competition robots were held together with tape. It was fragile between matches and couldn't survive RoboCup's handling.

Approach

Redesigned the shell around snap-fit and friction-fit joints so it assembles without tools, reworked geometry for additive manufacturing, and added ventilation for the electronics — coordinating directly with electrical and software.

Result

Tape-based assembly eliminated across the full six-robot fleet in about six weeks, with drivetrain and actuator assemblies redesigned alongside it.

RENDER PENDING
Shell + drivetrain assembly — upload coming soon
SHEET 02 OF 03 — TIKKUN OLAM MAKERS

Custom Assistive Reach Device

TEAMTOM Group Project
ROLEMechanical Engineer
TIMELINETeam Project
TOOLSSolidWorks, 3D Printing, Machining
Problem

Design, build, and prototype a device tailored to a specific challenge faced by a "need-knower" — a real person with a real, unmet need, not a hypothetical user.

Approach

Sketched front, back, and in-action hinge views to work out an armrest-mounted reach-and-hook mechanism, then modeled the plywood armrest, steel hinge, and hook hardware in SolidWorks before prototyping.

Result

A wearable, hinge-actuated reaching aid — built on a strict budget and refined through several rounds of rapid 3D-printed prototypes.

SHEET 03 OF 03 — ONGOING

Self-Serve Check-In Kiosk

COMPANYTAPN
ROLELead Product Engineering Intern
TIMELINEMay 2026 – Present
TOOLSSolidWorks, 3D Printing
Problem

Events needed self-serve check-in and registration hardware, but commercial kiosks run $2,000–$5,000+ — out of reach for the events TAPN serves.

Approach

Led end-to-end hardware development: designed the enclosure and casing in SolidWorks with precise tolerancing for manufacturability, then iterated quickly with 3D-printed prototypes to validate the form factor before committing to final production.

Result

A fully functional kiosk delivered in two months, for under $500 — a fraction of commercial equivalents.

RENDER PENDING
Enclosure + casing renders — upload coming soon
03 — MORE WORK

Additional builds.

Smaller projects and coursework that shaped how I design — shown here at a glance.

Individual Project

The '90s Naija Dresser

Culture-inspired 3D-printed shelf — my first hands-on project with GD&T, tolerancing, and design for manufacturing.

Individual Project

The Holiday Music Box

A hand-crank music box with a moving drawer mechanism — practice integrating motion geometry and electrical elements.

Personal Project

FIFA Club World Cup Trophy

Reverse-engineered and recreated the CWC trophy in SolidWorks + Blender, later featured in TAPN's augmented-reality platform.

Team Project — ME 2110

Mousetrap-Powered Robot

Built an automated pick-and-drop system from 5 mousetraps, 5 rubber bands, and a $120 budget. Ranked 16th of 56 teams as Testing Engineer.

Team Project

Budget Go-Kart for Kids

Designed the powertrain and transmission CAD models for a STEM-outreach go-kart, applying GD&T under a strict $150 budget.
